did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning approach, specifically proposing an optimum CNN classifier named OPT-MobileNet, to perform carcinogenic classification of human skin moles (benign vs. malignant). It compares this model against other neural networks like LeNet, MobileNet, and PyramidalNet using a dataset of skin mole slide images from Kaggle.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new deep learning model, OPT-MobileNet, for carcinogenic classification of skin moles. It includes a methodology section describing data collection from Kaggle, model architecture details, and an experimental results section comparing the proposed model against LeNet, MobileNet, and PyramidalNet with specific performance metrics (accuracy, loss, AUC). Although published in a conference proceedings (3ICT), it reports new technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: OPT-MobileNet: A Deep Learning Approach for Carcinogenic Classification of Human Skin mole
journal: 2023 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
brief_summary: This study proposes OPT-MobileNet, an optimized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifier for the carcinogenic classification of human skin moles into benign and malignant categories. The researchers collected microscopic slide images of skin moles from the Kaggle scientific archive, preprocessing them to 128x128 pixels and applying data augmentation and batch normalization. The proposed model was evaluated against state-of-the-art architectures including LeNet, MobileNet, and PyramidalNet using metrics such as accuracy, loss, and Area Under the Curve (AUC). Experimental results demonstrated that OPT-MobileNet achieved a top-1 accuracy of 0.9817 and an AUC of 0.8597, outperforming the comparative models. The findings indicate that the proposed deep learning approach effectively addresses the challenges of skin mole identification and can serve as a reliable decision support system in medical pathology.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as one of the two primary categories in the carcinogenic classification task using microscopic slide images of human skin moles.

Classified as the other primary category in the carcinogenic classification task. The paper explicitly links 'Melanoma' to 'cancerous skin mole' and notes it possesses structures like notched edge, inconsistent colour, and asymmetrical shape.
Mentioned as a third class in the multi-class definition of the classification process, alongside benign and malignant groups.

all_task_types.target: Carcinogenic classification of human skin moles into benign, malignant, and unknown classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes OPT-MobileNet for 'carcinogenic classification' of skin moles. The task involves distinguishing between benign and malignant (cancerous) skin moles, with a mention of an 'unknown' class. The model uses softmax activation for multi-class classification and evaluates performance using accuracy, loss, and AUC (Area Under the Curve) metrics on a dataset of microscopic slide images.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Scientific Archive (Human Skin Mole Slide Images)
datasets_used.description: A dataset of microscopic slide images of human skin moles collected from the Kaggle scientific archive. The images were preprocessed by converting 224x224 pixel images to 128x128 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the OPT-MobileNet, LeNet, MobileNet, and PyramidalNet models with data augmentation and batch normalization.
use.validation: Used for validation (e.g., OPT-MobileNet achieved 0.97 accuracy on the validation set with 50 epochs).
use.testing: Used for testing the final performance of the models (e.g., OPT-MobileNet achieved 0.9817 top-1 accuracy on the test set).

datasets_used.name: CIFAR-10
datasets_used.description: A well-known benchmark dataset used for state-of-the-art comparison of the CNN models (MobileNet, PyramidalNet, LeNet) in the study.

use.training: Used for comparative analysis of model performance (accuracy, loss, categorical accuracy) against the proposed skin mole classification task.
use.other: Used as a baseline benchmark for state-of-the-art comparison.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the performance of different CNN models (LeNet, MobileNet, PyramidNet, OPT-MobileNet) for carcinogenic classification of skin moles.
To assess classification performance, including true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives, for the compared models.
To numerically evaluate the classification performance of the models using specific formulas involving true positives, false positives, and false negatives.
To compare the performance of OPT-MobileNet against LeNet, MobileNet, and PyramidNet on the CIFAR-10 dataset and the skin mole dataset across varying epochs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy, loss, AUC, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

CIFAR-10.notes: MobileNet showed superior performance compared to LeNet and PyramidalNet on the CIFAR-10 dataset.

Skin Mole Slide Image Dataset.metric: AUC Value, Top-1 Accuracy, Loss, Parameters

LeNet.notes: Lowest performance; minimum prediction of true class and original false class.

MobileNet.notes: Best AUC value among baselines; maximum prediction of true class.

OPT-MobileNet.notes: Outperformed other models. Validation accuracy was 0.97 with 50 epochs and loss of 0.3.

Skin Mole Slide Image Dataset.notes: OPT-MobileNet achieved the highest top-1 accuracy (0.9817) and outperformed LeNet, MobileNet, and PyramidalNet. MobileNet had the best AUC (0.8597) among the baseline models.

architecture_details.description: Optimized version of MobileNet using depth-wise separable convolutions. It incorporates data preprocessing, augmentation, weight regularization, and batch normalization. The architecture consists of 39 layers.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named, but mentions 'optimization in classification time is achieved by fine tuning of hyper parameters'
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named, but loss value reported as 0.522

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of 224x224 pixel microscopic slide images to 128x128 pixels
Data augmentation including shearing, cropping, and rotating images
Batch normalization through weight regularization (L2 regularization)

conclusions: The proposed OPT-MobileNet model effectively addresses the challenges of carcinogenic classification of human skin moles, achieving a top-1 accuracy of 0.9817 and an AUC of 0.8597.
OPT-MobileNet outperformed comparative state-of-the-art models, including LeNet, MobileNet, and PyramidalNet, in terms of accuracy and efficiency for skin mole classification.

The study confirms that automated AI-based computer-aided diagnostics using deep learning can serve as a reliable Decision Support System (DSS) in medical pathology for identifying benign versus malignant skin moles.

The proposed method classifies skin mole types with high precision in an optimum amount of time, overcoming parameterized constraints associated with manual identification of microscopic slide images.

MobileNet architecture demonstrated superior performance compared to LeNet and PyramidalNet on the CIFAR-10 benchmark dataset, justifying its optimization into OPT-MobileNet for the specific dermatology task.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset of microscopic slide images collected from the Kaggle scientific archive, with no mention of external validation or multi-center data, limiting generalizability.
The paper does not report the total number of images, nor the specific split counts for training, validation, and testing sets, raising concerns about data leakage or insufficient sample size.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons between OPT-MobileNet and baseline models (LeNet, MobileNet, PyramidalNet) are based solely on point estimates of accuracy and AUC.
The model was trained on 128x128 pixel images, which is a significant reduction from the original 224x224 resolution, potentially losing critical diagnostic features in microscopic slide images.

The classification head uses 10 neurons for a task described as binary (benign vs. malignant) or ternary (benign, malignant, unknown), which is architecturally inconsistent and suggests potential misreporting or confusion in class definitions.
The paper acknowledges limitations regarding cloud-based deployment, specifically citing storage hurdles for complex situations with unlimited datasets, indicating scalability concerns.
The reported top-1 accuracy of 0.9817 for OPT-MobileNet is not accompanied by standard deviation or cross-validation results, making the robustness of this high performance uncertain.

The study compares the proposed model against LeNet, MobileNet, and PyramidalNet, but does not include more recent or state-of-the-art dermatology-specific models (e.g., ResNet, EfficientNet variants) in the primary comparison, limiting the context of 'state-of-the-art' claims.
